learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, HashMap, Time Complexity, Basic Counting
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine counting votes in an election where one candidate is guaranteed to receive more than half of all votes. Instead of counting every vote separately, the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm efficiently identifies the winner in one pass.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine counting votes in an election where one candidate is guaranteed to receive more than half of all votes. Instead of counting every vote separately, the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm efficiently identifies the winner in one pass.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm whenever a majority element (> n/2 occurrences) is guaranteed and constant space is required.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem guarantees an element appears more than half the time and asks for constant space, think of the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an array nums of size n, return the majority element. The majority element is the element that appears more than ⌊n / 2⌋ times. You may assume that the majority element always exists in the array.

examples.explanation: 3 appears 2 times, which is more than ⌊3/2⌋.
2 appears 4 times, which is greater than ⌊7/2⌋.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For each element, count its occurrences by scanning the entire array.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Whenever two different elements are encountered, they cancel each other out. Since the majority element appears more than half the time, it can never be completely eliminated, leaving it as the final candidate.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The majority element cannot be completely cancelled by all other elements.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem guarantees an element appears more than half the time and asks for constant space, think of the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Different elements cancel each other. Since the majority element appears more than half the time, it survives all cancellations and remains the final candidate.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem guarantees an element appears more than half the time and asks for constant space, think of the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm.
commonWrongPaths: Using sorting when O(1) space is expected.
Forgetting to reset the candidate when count becomes zero.
Confusing majority (> n/2) with most frequent.
Using HashMap when constant space is required.
Applying Boyer-Moore when no majority is guaranteed without verification.

hints: Maintain a candidate and a count.
The remaining candidate will be the majority.
commonMisconceptions: Using sorting when O(1) space is expected.
Forgetting to reset the candidate when count becomes zero.
Confusing majority (> n/2) with most frequent.
Using HashMap when constant space is required.
Applying Boyer-Moore when no majority is guaranteed without verification.

pattern.name: Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem guarantees an element appears more than half the time and asks for constant space, think of the Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm.

approaches.title: Count Every Element
approaches.idea: For each element, count its occurrences by scanning the entire array.
algorithm: Return the element whose count exceeds n/2.
approaches.dryRun: Repeatedly count occurrences for every element.

code.java: Nested loop solution omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: HashMap Frequency Counting
approaches.idea: Store the frequency of each element using a HashMap and return the element whose frequency exceeds n/2.
algorithm: Count the frequency of each element.
Return the element with frequency greater than n/2.
approaches.dryRun: Increment frequencies while traversing the array.

approaches.title: Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm
approaches.idea: Maintain a candidate and a counter. If the counter becomes zero, choose the current element as the new candidate. Increase the counter when the current element matches the candidate; otherwise decrease it. The final candidate is the majority element.
approaches.intuition: Different elements cancel each other. Since the majority element appears more than half the time, it survives all cancellations and remains the final candidate.
algorithm: If count becomes zero, set current element as candidate.
If current element equals candidate, increment count.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[2,2,1,1,1,2,2]
Candidate=2 Count=1
2→Count=2
1→Count=1
1→Count=0
1→Candidate=1 Count=1
2→Count=0
2→Candidate=2 Count=1
Answer=2

visualization.description: Different elements cancel each other, leaving only the majority element.
steps: Increase count for the same element.
Decrease count for a different element.
Reset candidate when count becomes zero.

revisionNotes: Reset candidate when count becomes zero.

interviewFollowUps: Why does the cancellation idea work?
What if no majority element exists?
How would you verify the candidate?
Can this be extended to elements occurring more than n/3 times?
interviewTips: State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The majority element cannot be completely cancelled by all other elements.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Candidate → Count → Cancel Different Elements → Return Candidate.

importantPoints: Candidate changes only when count becomes zero.
commonMistakes: Using sorting when O(1) space is expected.
Forgetting to reset the candidate when count becomes zero.
Confusing majority (> n/2) with most frequent.
Using HashMap when constant space is required.
Applying Boyer-Moore when no majority is guaranteed without verification.
